idex.name: IDEX v1
idex.desc: IDEX launched in October 2017 as the first hybrid DEX combining off-chain order matching with onchain settlement. Originally branded as Aurora, it grew to process over 4 million orders per day during the ICO boom. IDEX v1 was superseded by v2 in fall 2020.

etherdelta.name: EtherDelta v2
etherdelta.desc: EtherDelta launched July 12, 2016 as the first widely-used DEX for ERC-20 tokens. It became the go-to venue for ICO token trading in 2017, peaking at ~$10M daily volume. On December 20, 2017, attackers hijacked its DNS to steal $1.4M from users. In November 2018, the SEC charged its creator with operating an unregistered exchange — the first such action against a DEX.

tokenstore.name: Token.Store
tokenstore.desc: Launched July 30, 2017 as a fully onchain DEX with both onchain order matching and settlement. Processed over 500,000 transactions across 100,000+ accounts and later expanded to EOS. The anonymous team shut it down on June 22, 2020, publishing a farewell citing the rise of AMMs like Uniswap as having made order-book DEXs obsolete.

etherdelta_m1.name: EtherDelta v1
etherdelta_m1.desc: An earlier iteration of the EtherDelta exchange (December 2016). Multiple contract versions were deployed, each requiring users to manually withdraw and redeposit. Superseded by the well-known v2 contract at 0x8d12A197.

singularx.name: SingularX
singularx.desc: SingularX (by SingularDTV) launched November 2017 as a DEX for their blockchain entertainment economy. Based on EtherDelta's architecture, it saw over $3M in 24h volume during the CENNZ listing. SingularDTV rebranded to Breaker in 2019 but failed to deliver on its decentralized Netflix vision.

enclaves.name: Unknown DEX
enclaves.desc: An unverified EtherDelta-style DEX contract from September 2017. The protocol behind this contract has not been identified — the source code is not published on Etherscan but follows the standard deposit/withdraw pattern.

decentrex.name: Decentrex
decentrex.desc: An EtherDelta-fork DEX that was notably served directly from GitHub Pages (circa 2017). Minimal presence online — one of many EtherDelta forks from the 2017-2018 era that attracted small trading communities.

joyso.name: Joyso
joyso.desc: Launched in Taiwan, May 2018 after a March 2018 ICO, combining off-chain matching with onchain settlement. Partnered with CoolBitX to offer the first cold-storage DEX trading experience.

ethen.name: ETHEN
ethen.desc: A decentralized exchange for ERC-20 tokens, founded in 2018 and headquartered in Saint Petersburg, Russia. Used a custom contract design with a direct balances mapping rather than the standard EtherDelta token-tracking pattern.

bitcratic.name: Bitcratic
bitcratic.desc: An EtherDelta-style DEX with off-chain orderbook and onchain settlement (2018). One of the more active EtherDelta forks during the post-ICO trading boom.

etherc.name: EtherC
etherc.desc: Claimed to be the first DEX with gas-free trading via its ETHERCToken (EET) reimbursement system (2018). An unusual approach where gas costs were subsidized through the platform's own token.

enclavesdex.name: EnclavesDex
enclavesdex.desc: A DEX and early liquidity aggregator on Ethereum (June 2018) that routed trades through both its own order book and EtherDelta. An early precursor to the aggregator model later popularized by 1inch.

ens_old.name: ENS Old Registrar
ens_old.desc: The original .eth registrar launched May 4, 2017 (Star Wars Day) after an earlier March launch was aborted due to a bug. Used Vickrey sealed-bid auctions where winning bids were locked in deed contracts. Replaced by the permanent registrar on May 4, 2019; names not migrated by May 2020 returned to the pool, but deed deposits can still be reclaimed via releaseDeed().

confideal.name: Confideal
confideal.desc: Confideal was a smart contract platform letting non-technical users create and manage escrow deals on Ethereum without coding. Conducted an ICO in November 2017 for the CDL token, with a Bittrex listing deal. Included an arbitration module for dispute resolution. Failed to gain adoption and went inactive by 2019.

dada.name: DADA Collectible
dada.desc: A collaborative digital art platform. Deployed its "Creeps & Weirdos" NFT collection on October 5, 2017 — after CryptoPunks but before CryptoKitties. Featured 108 unique pieces by 30 artists and was the first NFT project to implement artist royalties coded directly into the smart contract, using a modified CryptoPunks contract.

mooncatrescue.name: MoonCatRescue
mooncatrescue.desc: Deployed August 9, 2017, one of the earliest NFTs on Ethereum — before the term "NFT" or ERC-721 existed. Features 25,440 generative pixel art cats with a proof-of-work minting mechanism. Only 3,365 cats were rescued in 2017; on March 12, 2021 crypto-archaeologists rediscovered it and all remaining 19,000+ cats were rescued within hours. Note: ~100 ETH from the genesis cat is permanently stuck at address(0) due to a contract bug. Users with pending adoption requests must cancel them to reclaim escrowed ETH.

fomo3d_long.name: Fomo3D Long
fomo3d_long.desc: Players buy keys that reset a countdown timer; the last buyer wins the jackpot. Round 1 ended August 2018 paying 10,469 ETH (~$2.8M), won via a block-stuffing attack that prevented competing transactions. At peak, the contract held over $43M. Note: the isHuman modifier means smart contract wallets cannot withdraw — only EOAs.

fomo3d_quick.name: Fomo3D Quick
fomo3d_quick.desc: A faster-round variant of Fomo3D (2018) with shorter countdown durations. Same key-purchase mechanic and dividend system as Fomo3D Long. Note: isHuman modifier — smart contract wallets cannot withdraw.

fomo3d_short.name: Fomo3D Short
fomo3d_short.desc: Another variant of Fomo3D (2018). Same core mechanics — key purchases, countdown timer, dividend distribution. Note: isHuman modifier — smart contract wallets cannot withdraw.

etherdelta_m0.name: EtherDelta v0
etherdelta_m0.desc: The earliest EtherDelta contract, deployed around August 2016 using Solidity v0.3.6 — one of the very first DEX contracts on Ethereum, deployed just months after the DAO hack. The team iterated through multiple versions (Oct 2016, Feb 2017) before the well-known v2; each upgrade required manual migration.

neufund.name: Neufund EtherToken
neufund.desc: Founded in 2016 in Berlin, Neufund conducted the world's first public offering of tokenized equity on Ethereum in November 2018. Deployed over EUR 20M in capital for 11,000 investors from 123 countries; flagship case Greyp Bikes completed the full cycle from tokenization to Porsche acquisition. Shut down January 17, 2022 after years of regulatory uncertainty with BaFin.

neufund_locked.name: Neufund
neufund_locked.desc: The ICBM (Initial Capital Building Mechanism) escrow contract where Neufund investors locked ETH in exchange for Neumark (NEU) tokens. All 540-day lock periods expired in 2019. To recover ETH: approveAndCall on NEU (unlocks + returns ETH-T in one tx), then withdraw ETH-T to raw ETH.

bancor_eth.name: Bancor Old ETH Token
bancor_eth.desc: Bancor launched June 2017 with a then-record $153M ICO and deployed the first-ever automated market maker contracts on Ethereum, inventing bonding curves and liquidity pool tokens before "DeFi" was coined. On July 9, 2018, attackers stole 25,000 ETH (~$12.5M) via a compromised upgrade wallet. This is the legacy ETH wrapper from Bancor's early architecture.

switchdex.name: SwitchDex
switchdex.desc: An EtherDelta-fork DEX by Switch.ag (2019), notable for being active from 2019-2024 with 8,000+ trades — unusually long-lived for an order-book DEX in the AMM era.

coinchangex.name: Coinchangex
coinchangex.desc: An EtherDelta-fork DEX (2018) that shut down around March 2020. Processed a moderate volume of trades during the ICO token trading era.

swisscryptoexchange.name: SwissCryptoExchange
swisscryptoexchange.desc: A whitelisted EtherDelta-fork DEX (2018). Unusual in that deposits required whitelist approval, but the withdraw function is permissionless — anyone with a balance can withdraw.

lscx.name: LSCX
lscx.desc: The DEX component of Lescovex, a Spanish platform for issuing and trading Ethereum financial contracts (October 2018). Part of a broader ecosystem that included tokenized securities and smart contract templates.

bitcratic_v1.name: Bitcratic v1
bitcratic_v1.desc: The original Bitcratic contract before the team migrated to a newer version. Users who didn't move their funds to the updated contract still have balances here.

marketplace.name: MarketPlace
marketplace.desc: An EtherDelta-fork DEX from the 2017-2018 era. One of dozens of exchanges that cloned EtherDelta's open-source smart contract to launch competing trading platforms.

bitox.name: Bitox
bitox.desc: An EtherDelta-fork DEX that also offered airdrop aggregation services (2018). Combined token trading with a directory of active ERC-20 airdrops.

ed_fork_6_9eth.name: ED Fork (0xc513)
ed_fork_6_9eth.desc: An unverified EtherDelta v2 fork — source code not published on Etherscan but bytecode matches the standard pattern.

ethernext.name: Ethernext
ethernext.desc: An EtherDelta-fork DEX from the 2017-2018 era. One of dozens of exchanges that cloned EtherDelta's open-source smart contract to launch competing trading platforms.

seeddex_v2.name: SeedDex v2
seeddex_v2.desc: An EtherDelta-fork DEX from the 2017-2018 era. One of dozens of exchanges that cloned EtherDelta's open-source smart contract to launch competing trading platforms.

seeddex_v3.name: SeedDex v3
seeddex_v3.desc: An EtherDelta-fork DEX from the 2017-2018 era. One of dozens of exchanges that cloned EtherDelta's open-source smart contract to launch competing trading platforms.

polarisdex.name: PolarisDEX
polarisdex.desc: An EtherDelta-fork DEX from the 2017-2018 era. One of dozens of exchanges that cloned EtherDelta's open-source smart contract to launch competing trading platforms.

ethmall.name: Ethmall
ethmall.desc: An EtherDelta-fork DEX from the 2017-2018 era. One of dozens of exchanges that cloned EtherDelta's open-source smart contract to launch competing trading platforms.

extoke.name: ExToke
extoke.desc: A fee-free DEX for ERC-20 tokens (2018) that planned to add an ICO launchpad feature. Conducted its own token sale but failed to gain traction.

algodex.name: AlgoDEX
algodex.desc: An EtherDelta-fork DEX from the 2017-2018 era. One of dozens of exchanges that cloned EtherDelta's open-source smart contract to launch competing trading platforms.

ndex.name: nDEx
ndex.desc: An EtherDelta-fork DEX from the 2017-2018 era. One of dozens of exchanges that cloned EtherDelta's open-source smart contract to launch competing trading platforms.

edex.name: EDex
edex.desc: An EtherDelta-fork DEX from the 2017-2018 era. One of dozens of exchanges that cloned EtherDelta's open-source smart contract to launch competing trading platforms.

tradexone.name: TradexOne
tradexone.desc: An EtherDelta-fork DEX from the 2017-2018 era. One of dozens of exchanges that cloned EtherDelta's open-source smart contract to launch competing trading platforms.

afrodex.name: Afrodex
afrodex.desc: An EtherDelta-fork DEX from the 2017-2018 era. One of dozens of exchanges that cloned EtherDelta's open-source smart contract to launch competing trading platforms.

readyplayerone.name: ReadyPlayerONE
readyplayerone.desc: Deployed July 2018, a Fomo3D fork named after the Ernest Cline novel and Spielberg film. Same key-purchase countdown mechanic where the last buyer before the timer expires wins the pot. Note: isHuman modifier — smart contract wallets cannot withdraw.

fomo3d_lightning.name: FoMo3D Lightning
fomo3d_lightning.desc: One of the earliest Fomo3D clones, deployed July 2018 within weeks of the original. A speed-round variant of the countdown-timer game where rounds resolved faster than standard Fomo3D. Note: isHuman modifier — smart contract wallets cannot withdraw.

etherdelta_v3.name: Etheropt Old
etherdelta_v3.desc: The first decentralized options exchange on Ethereum (early 2016), from the creator of EtherDelta. Offered vanilla call and put options on ETH/USD using Poloniex and Coindesk data verified by Reality Keys. Entirely autonomous with no owner and no fees. Shut down in late 2016.

gandhiji.name: GandhiJi
gandhiji.desc: was a P3D clone (2018) themed around Indian independence. Direct fork of PoWH3D with identical 10% buy/sell fee mechanics. 8,800+ holders. Fully autonomous, no owner.

zethr.name: Zethr
zethr.desc: was a P3D-style casino token (2018) where holders "be the house." Users pick dividend rates (2-33%). Includes slots and other casino games. This is the token contract holding user dividends.

zethr_main.name: Zethr Casino
zethr_main.desc: was the main Zethr casino bankroll contract (2018). Holds house funds and player dividends from casino games.

ethpyramid.name: EthPyramid
ethpyramid.desc: was the original Ethereum dividend/pyramid token (early 2018), predating PoWH3D. Later rebranded to EthPhoenix.

fomogame.name: FoMoGame
fomogame.desc: was a Fomo3D clone (2018). Same countdown/key-buying mechanics with player vaults.

powh3d.name: PoWH3D
powh3d.desc: A self-aware Ponzi-style token game (February 2018) rewritten after the original PoWH was drained of 200+ ETH via an integer overflow bug. Every buy and sell of P3D tokens incurs a 10% fee distributed to all existing holders. At peak held 16,000 ETH (~$6.4M). Spawned thousands of forks including Fomo3D.

powm.name: POWM
powm.desc: Proof of Weak Math, deployed April 2018 during the first wave of P3D clones. Named as a jab at PoWL, an earlier clone with a coding error that broke its dividend math. Featured a 20% fee and briefly held over 4,000 ETH.

pooh.name: POOH
pooh.desc: Deployed April 2018, POOH branded itself as "the most honest P3D clone" and an "honest proof of cloning." Same 10% buy/sell fee mechanics as PoWH3D, hosted at number2.io.

powtf.name: PoWTF
powtf.desc: Short for "Proof of World Trade Francs," deployed April 2018. Named after a fictional stablecoin jokingly proposed by Vitalik Buterin. Featured higher fees than standard P3D: 20% on buys and 25% on sells.

powh_clone1.name: Hourglass Clone A
powh_clone1.desc: An unnamed PoWH3D fork deployed April 2018, using the same Hourglass dividend token contract with no meaningful modifications. One of dozens of near-identical clones launched during the P3D craze.

powh_clone2.name: Hourglass Clone B
powh_clone2.desc: Another unbranded Hourglass contract deployed April 2018. A direct copy of the PoWH3D source code with identical 10% buy/sell fees distributed to token holders.

lockedin.name: LOCKEDiN
lockedin.desc: Deployed April 2018, LOCKEDiN was a PoWH3D fork that emphasized the "lock in your ETH for dividends" angle. Standard Hourglass mechanics where every transaction fee is split among all existing token holders.

stronghold.name: StrongHold
stronghold.desc: A PoWH3D clone from April 2018 that branded itself as a safer, more resilient version of the P3D dividend model. Used the same autonomous contract mechanics with no owner and no self-destruct.

powh_clone3.name: Hourglass Clone C
powh_clone3.desc: Deployed April 2018 at the peak of the PoWH3D clone wave. An unmodified copy of the Hourglass dividend contract where holding tokens earns a share of all future buy and sell fees.

powh_clone4.name: Hourglass Clone D
powh_clone4.desc: Yet another April 2018 Hourglass fork, part of the rapid proliferation of P3D clones that flooded Ethereum in spring 2018. Identical dividend token mechanics with 10% fees on every transaction.

unkoin.name: UnKoin
unkoin.desc: A PoWH3D clone from April 2018 with a play on "unknown coin." Standard Hourglass dividend mechanics where ETH deposits mint tokens and all trading activity generates passive income for holders.

acedapp.name: AceDapp
acedapp.desc: Deployed October 2019, a late-era PoWH3D clone that launched well after most P3D forks had already lost their user bases. Standard Hourglass dividend mechanics with 10% buy/sell fees.

cryptominertoken.name: CryptoMinerToken
cryptominertoken.desc: was a dividend-yielding PoWH3D clone (September 2018) hosted at minertoken.cloud, with customized fee tiers: 10% deposit, 4% withdrawal, 1% transfer, and 33% referral rewards.

bluechip.name: BlueChip
bluechip.desc: was a PoWH3D clone (September 2019) implementing the standard 10% fee-sharing dividend mechanism where trading activity generates ETH rewards for token holders.

rev1.name: REV1
rev1.desc: Deployed May 2018, REV1 was an early PoWH3D fork from the second month of the P3D clone wars. Straightforward dividend token where 10% of every buy and sell is redistributed to holders.

potj.name: POTJ
potj.desc: Proof of Trevon James, launched April 2018, was a PoWH3D fork with higher fees (20% buys, 25% sells) themed around a crypto YouTube personality involved in the BitConnect case.

lynia.name: LYNIA
lynia.desc: A gaming and gambling platform deployed August 2018 that combined PoWH3D-style dividend tokens with provably fair on-chain games. Has an isHuman modifier — smart contract wallets cannot withdraw.

blackgold.name: BlackGoldEthereum
blackgold.desc: was a dividend-based PoWH3D clone (July 2020) designed to provide passive income to token holders through a buy/sell/reinvestment mechanism. The project website blackgoldethereum.club is no longer accessible.

proofofcraiggrant.name: ProofOfCraigGrant
proofofcraiggrant.desc: Deployed April 2018, a meme-themed PoWH3D fork named after Craig Grant, one of BitConnect's most prominent YouTube promoters. Part of a series of personality-themed P3D clones alongside Proof of Trevon James.

sportcrypt.name: SportCrypt
sportcrypt.desc: was a peer-to-peer decentralized sports betting exchange (January 2018) with zero fees and no KYC requirements. Later rebranded to Degens and received a MakerDAO community grant to accept DAI alongside ETH.

dailydivs.name: DailyDivs
dailydivs.desc: was a PoWH3D clone (October 2018) offering daily dividend distributions to token holders through a 10% buy/sell fee. Has an isHuman modifier — smart contract wallets cannot withdraw.

proofofcommunity.name: ProofOfCommunity
proofofcommunity.desc: A May 2018 PoWH3D fork that rebranded the dividend token concept around community ownership. Same underlying Hourglass mechanics where all transaction fees are split among existing holders.

bitconnect_powh.name: BitConnect Token
bitconnect_powh.desc: Deployed June 2019, a PoWH3D clone that borrowed the BitConnect name a year and a half after the original lending platform collapsed. Standard dividend token mechanics; unrelated to the actual BitConnect project.

eightherbank.name: Eightherbank
eightherbank.desc: A November 2019 PoWH3D fork with a banking theme. Uses the Hourglass dividend model with an isHuman modifier — smart contract wallets cannot withdraw.

nexgen.name: Nexgen
nexgen.desc: Deployed June 2019, a PoWH3D clone that positioned itself as a next-generation dividend token. Identical Hourglass mechanics to P3D with 10% fees on buys and sells.

diamonddividend.name: DiamondDividend
diamonddividend.desc: A November 2019 PoWH3D fork using diamond-themed branding for the standard Hourglass dividend model. Holders earn ETH from all future transaction fees proportional to their token balance.

e25.name: E25 Booster
e25.desc: Deployed February 2019, a PoWH3D clone with a modified fee structure hinted at by its "25" branding. Has an isHuman modifier — smart contract wallets cannot withdraw.

bitconnect2.name: BitConnect v2
bitconnect2.desc: The second in a series of BitConnect-themed PoWH3D clones, deployed June 2019. Reused the infamous brand name purely for attention; standard P3D dividend token under the hood.

ethplatinum.name: ETHPlatinum
ethplatinum.desc: Deployed November 2021, one of the last PoWH3D clones ever created on Ethereum mainnet. By this point gas fees made the P3D dividend model largely impractical for small deposits.

divsnetwork.name: DivsNetwork
divsnetwork.desc: A July 2020 PoWH3D fork that framed itself as a dividend distribution network. Fully autonomous contract with no owner, running the same Hourglass token model as every other P3D clone.

ethercenter.name: EtherCenter
ethercenter.desc: Deployed July 2019, EtherCenter was a PoWH3D fork that added no meaningful features over the original. Standard 10% fee on every transaction, distributed pro rata to all token holders.

redchip.name: RedChip
redchip.desc: An October 2019 PoWH3D clone that borrowed stock market terminology — "red chip" refers to mainland China companies listed in Hong Kong. Standard Hourglass dividend mechanics beneath the branding.

cxxmain.name: CxxMain
cxxmain.desc: Deployed November 2019 with a cryptic name that may reference C++ programming. A PoWH3D fork with unmodified Hourglass contract code and the same fee-redistribution mechanics.

familyonly.name: FamilyOnlyToken
familyonly.desc: An August 2020 PoWH3D fork with an invitation-only theme suggested by its name. Same Hourglass dividend model where ETH deposits mint tokens and every trade generates passive income for all holders.

spw.name: SPW
spw.desc: Deployed June 2020, SPW was a minimal PoWH3D clone with no discernible branding or website. Pure Hourglass dividend contract mechanics.

ethdiamond.name: ETHDIAMOND
ethdiamond.desc: A July 2020 PoWH3D clone using diamond-hands imagery. Identical dividend token contract to P3D — buy and sell fees are pooled and shared among all token holders automatically.

ethershares.name: Ethershares
ethershares.desc: Deployed December 2018, Ethershares framed P3D-style token ownership as "shares" in an ETH revenue pool. Standard Hourglass mechanics with no technical differences from the original.

twelvehour.name: TwelveHourToken
twelvehour.desc: An October 2018 PoWH3D fork, possibly named to suggest rapid dividend accumulation. Uses the same Hourglass contract where transaction fees are continuously redistributed to token holders.

neutrino81.name: Neutrino81
neutrino81.desc: Deployed March 2019 with a physics-inspired name. A PoWH3D fork using the same autonomous dividend token contract — no owner, no admin keys, just the Hourglass buy/sell fee loop.

hourglassx.name: HourglassX
hourglassx.desc: A December 2018 PoWH3D fork that wore the "Hourglass" name openly — the original P3D contract was called Hourglass internally. Standard dividend mechanics with 10% transaction fees.

fairexchange.name: FairExchange
fairexchange.desc: Deployed October 2018, FairExchange pitched the PoWH3D dividend model as a "fair" alternative to centralized exchanges. Under the surface, identical Hourglass contract code.

pomda.name: POMDA
pomda.desc: A June 2018 PoWH3D fork whose name likely riffs on "Proof of Mass Dividend Accumulation" or similar. Vanilla Hourglass contract — ETH in, tokens out, dividends from all activity.

decentether.name: DecentEther
decentether.desc: Deployed August 2020 during DeFi Summer, though it had nothing to do with DeFi — just another PoWH3D clone running the old Hourglass dividend model on a chain now dominated by Uniswap and Compound.

bitconnect3.name: BitConnect v3
bitconnect3.desc: The third BitConnect-branded PoWH3D fork, deployed October 2019. By this point the BitConnect name was the subject of SEC enforcement actions, but clone deployers kept reusing it.

furious.name: Furious
furious.desc: A July 2020 PoWH3D clone with an aggressive name but stock-standard mechanics. Same autonomous Hourglass contract where buy/sell fees are distributed to all token holders in perpetuity.

etherdiamond.name: EtherDiamond
etherdiamond.desc: Deployed July 2020, a sibling to ETHDIAMOND with nearly identical branding. Both are straight PoWH3D forks where token holders earn a cut of every future transaction.

powh_clone5.name: Hourglass Clone E
powh_clone5.desc: An October 2019 unnamed Hourglass fork — one of several unbranded P3D copies that were deployed with no website or community, just the raw contract on Ethereum.

cryptosurge.name: CryptoSurge
cryptosurge.desc: Deployed October 2019 with a name evoking price surges. A PoWH3D fork using the proven Hourglass mechanics where buying tokens costs a 10% fee that goes straight to existing holders.

hourglass_clone6.name: Hourglass Clone F
hourglass_clone6.desc: A November 2018 unbranded Hourglass contract. Deployed during the crypto winter bear market when ETH had fallen over 90% from its peak, yet P3D clones were still appearing.

upower.name: UPower
upower.desc: Deployed July 2018, UPower was a PoWH3D fork from the summer of the P3D craze. Standard dividend token — deposit ETH, receive tokens, earn from every future transaction on the contract.

hourglass_clone7.name: Hourglass Clone G
hourglass_clone7.desc: A May 2018 unbranded Hourglass fork, one of the earliest nameless P3D copies. Deployed during the peak month of clone activity when dozens of identical contracts appeared on Ethereum.

redchip2.name: RedChip v2
redchip2.desc: The second iteration of RedChip, deployed October 2019 alongside its predecessor. Another PoWH3D fork with the same dividend token mechanics repackaged under a stock-market-inspired name.

omnidex.name: OmniDex
omnidex.desc: Deployed August 2018, OmniDex distinguished itself with 18% dividends, masternodes, and 0% transfer fees — tweaking the standard P3D formula. Despite the "DEX" name, it was a dividend token, not an exchange.

spw2.name: SPW v2
spw2.desc: The second SPW contract, deployed September 2020. A relaunch of the original SPW using the same PoWH3D Hourglass mechanics with no apparent changes.

bounties.name: Bounties Network
bounties.desc: was a decentralized bounty platform (December 2017) built by ConsenSys for open-source work and freelance tasks on Ethereum. Bounty issuers who never killed or fulfilled their bounties still have ETH locked in the StandardBounties v1 contract.

ageofdinos.name: Age of Dinos
ageofdinos.desc: was an NFT Dutch auction (2024). Bidders who overpaid above the clearing price have unclaimed ETH refunds available via claimAndRefund().

personabid.name: PersonaBid
personabid.desc: was an NFT raffle auction (2024). Bidders who were not selected or overbid have unclaimed ETH refunds available via claimAndRefund().

maker_weth.name: Maker W-ETH
maker_weth.desc: The original Wrapped Ether contract predating the canonical WETH9. Solved the fundamental problem that native ETH couldn't be used in ERC-20 smart contracts, enabling early DEXs like OasisDEX and Radar Relay. Deprecated when the community converged on WETH9 in late 2017.

old_weth.name: Old WETH
old_weth.desc: An early Wrapped Ether contract deployed in June 2016, one of the first attempts at wrapping native ETH into an ERC-20 token. Users deposited ETH and received WETH tokens 1:1. Predates both Maker W-ETH and the canonical WETH9. Over 3,200 ETH remains wrapped by depositors who never unwrapped.

lastwinner.name: Last Winner
lastwinner.desc: A Fomo3D-style gambling game deployed in 2018, also known as "Last Winner" — one of the largest Fomo3D clones by total ETH volume. Players purchase keys that extend a countdown timer; when the timer runs out, the last buyer wins the pot. Unclaimed dividends and affiliate earnings remain withdrawable. Note: isHuman modifier — smart contract wallets cannot withdraw.

digixdao.name: DigixDAO
digixdao.desc: DigixDAO launched in March 2016 as one of Ethereum's first DAOs, raising 466,648 ETH in a crowdsale that filled its hard cap in just 12 hours. The DAO funded development of Digix's gold-backed DGX token, but after years of limited adoption, token holders voted 97% in favor of dissolution via Project Ragnarok in January 2020. The Acid refund contract was deployed in March 2020, allowing any DGD holder to permanently burn their tokens for a pro-rata share of the treasury at roughly 0.193 ETH per DGD.

p4d.name: P4D
p4d.desc: A PoWH3D clone from 2018. Standard Hourglass dividend mechanics where ETH deposits mint tokens and trading fees generate passive income for holders.

nucypher_worklock.name: NuCypher WorkLock
nucypher_worklock.desc: NuCypher launched a WorkLock distribution in September 2020, where participants deposited ETH to receive NU tokens. ETH was refundable after completing staking work. NuCypher merged with Keep Network to form Threshold Network in 2022, and the staking requirement was removed — all participants now qualify for a full refund regardless of work completed.

tessera_dead.name: Fractional DEAD Vault
tessera_dead.desc: Fractional Art (later Tessera) fractionalized NFTs into ERC-20 tokens. When a buyout auction completed, fraction holders could burn tokens for proportional ETH via cash(). Tessera shut down in May 2023; the frontend is dead but contracts remain functional.

tessera_sweep.name: Fractional SWEEP Vault
tessera_sweep.desc: Fractional Art (later Tessera) fractionalized NFTs into ERC-20 tokens. When a buyout auction completed, fraction holders could burn tokens for proportional ETH via cash(). Tessera shut down in May 2023; the frontend is dead but contracts remain functional.

tessera_zcat.name: Fractional ZCAT Vault
tessera_zcat.desc: Fractional Art (later Tessera) fractionalized NFTs into ERC-20 tokens. When a buyout auction completed, fraction holders could burn tokens for proportional ETH via cash(). Tessera shut down in May 2023; the frontend is dead but contracts remain functional.

kyber_feehandler.name: Kyber FeeHandler
kyber_feehandler.desc: Kyber Network v1 (Katalyst) distributed ETH trading fees to KNC stakers who voted in governance epochs. Kyber has since migrated to KyberSwap with a different architecture. The old staking UI is dead, but the FeeHandler contract still holds unclaimed epoch rewards with no expiration.

thedao.name: The DAO
thedao.desc: The DAO launched in April 2016 as the largest crowdfund in history, raising 11.5 million ETH from over 20,000 investors. In June 2016, a reentrancy exploit drained 3.6M ETH, leading to the contentious Ethereum hard fork that split ETH and ETC. After the fork restored the funds, a WithdrawDAO wrapper contract was deployed, allowing any DAO token holder to burn their tokens 1:1 for ETH. Over 81,000 ETH remains unclaimed in the wrapper to this day.

augur_v1.name: Augur v1
augur_v1.desc: Augur launched in July 2018 as Ethereum's first decentralized prediction market protocol. Users could create markets on real-world events, trade outcome shares, and earn fees as market creators. The protocol migrated to v2 in August 2020, but 771 ETH remains locked in the original Cash contract backing unfilled orders, unclaimed market creator fees in mailbox contracts, and unredeemed winning shares from resolved markets.

ethfinex.name: Ethfinex Trustless
ethfinex.desc: Ethfinex Trustless launched in September 2018 as a non-custodial DEX backed by Bitfinex. Users deposited ETH into WrapperLockEth contracts, receiving ETHW tokens for off-chain order matching via the 0x protocol. The platform rebranded to DeversiFi in 2019, then migrated to StarkEx L2 in 2020, and rebranded again to rhino.fi in 2022. The original wrapper contracts are abandoned but fully functional — all time locks expired years ago, so depositors can withdraw directly without any third-party signature.

ethfinex_v2.name: Ethfinex Trustless v2
ethfinex_v2.desc: Ethfinex Trustless launched in September 2018 as a non-custodial DEX backed by Bitfinex. Users deposited ETH into WrapperLockEth contracts, receiving ETHW tokens for off-chain order matching via the 0x protocol. The platform rebranded to DeversiFi in 2019, then migrated to StarkEx L2 in 2020, and rebranded again to rhino.fi in 2022. The original wrapper contracts are abandoned but fully functional — all time locks expired years ago, so depositors can withdraw directly without any third-party signature.

switcheo_v2.name: Switcheo BrokerV2
switcheo_v2.desc: Switcheo Network launched in 2019 as a cross-chain DEX supporting Ethereum, NEO, and EOS. The BrokerV2 contract combined off-chain order matching with onchain settlement, similar to IDEX. The project pivoted to Switcheo TradeHub (a Cosmos-based DEX) in 2020, then rebranded to Carbon in 2021. The Ethereum broker contract is abandoned but fully functional — users can withdraw via a 2-step announceWithdraw + slowWithdraw flow with zero delay.

unknown_dex_5995.name: Unknown DEX (0x5995)
unknown_dex_5995.desc: An anonymous EtherDelta-style order-book DEX deployed in July 2019. Featured deposit/withdraw for ETH and ERC-20 tokens, off-chain order matching, and onchain settlement. The contract is unverified with no known website or team. Activity ceased in March 2020 after ~30,000 transactions across 1,400 users. Standard withdraw function remains fully operational.

avastars.name: Avastars
avastars.desc: Avastars was an NFT project (2020) that allowed users to mint generative avatar NFTs. Users deposited ETH into the minting contract as a prepayment balance, which was drawn down as they minted. The project is defunct since September 2021, but the minting contract still holds unspent deposit balances. Users can withdraw their remaining deposits via withdrawDepositorBalance() with no restrictions.

aave_v1.name: Aave v1
aave_v1.desc: Aave v1 launched in January 2020 as the first version of the Aave lending protocol. Users deposited ETH and received aETH interest-bearing tokens that automatically accrued yield. The protocol migrated to v2 in December 2020 and v3 in March 2022, but the original UI dropped v1 support. aETH token holders can still redeem for ETH by calling redeem() on the aETH contract.

vlb.name: VLB Token
vlb.desc: Velix.AI's December 2017 ICO via the OpenZeppelin RefundableCrowdsale pattern. The sale missed its 5,000 ETH soft cap (raised ~4,900 ETH), the owner finalized into refund mode, and most depositors clawed back over the following months. 81 ETH still sits in the RefundVault waiting for the long tail of stragglers. Single-tx claim: call claimRefund() on the crowdsale, which delegates to the vault and returns your original deposit.

vlb_lino.name: VLB / Lino
vlb_lino.desc: A Wings-incubated 2018 ICO that reused the same VLB-style RefundableCrowdsale code. Soft cap (USD-denominated) was missed, the contract finalized into refund mode, and 17 ETH remained in the RefundVault unclaimed. Same single-tx pattern: claimRefund() on the crowdsale returns your deposit through the sibling vault.

fnt.name: Friend Network Token
fnt.desc: FriendNetwork's 2018 ICO with a 25,000 ETH minimum cap. Only ~3,700 ETH was raised, finalize() routed the contract into refund mode, and 16 ETH still sits in the RefundVault for buyers who never came back to claim. Standard OZ pattern: call claimRefund() on the crowdsale and the vault sends your original ETH back.

trend.name: Trend (TND)
trend.desc: Trend (TND) ran a 2018 ICO via the OpenZeppelin RefundableCrowdsale + CryptoHuntIco template. Soft cap missed, the contract finalized into refund mode, and ~28 ETH still sits in the sibling RefundVault. Single-tx claim: claimRefund() on the crowdsale returns your original deposit.

global_ico.name: Global ICO Token (GLIF)
global_ico.desc: Global ICO Token (GLIF) reused the OpenZeppelin RefundableCrowdsale + CryptoHuntIco template for its 2018 sale. Soft cap missed (raised ~896 ETH, refunded most), ~16 ETH still sits unclaimed in the sibling RefundVault. Standard claimRefund() returns your deposit.

crypto_hunt.name: CryptoHunt (CH)
crypto_hunt.desc: CryptoHunt is a blockchain treasure-hunting game whose 2018 ICO didn't reach its soft cap. The contract is the canonical CryptoHuntIco source — OpenZeppelin RefundableCrowdsale pattern, public claimRefund() routing to the sibling RefundVault. ~10 ETH still claimable.

alttradex.name: Alttradex (ATXT)
alttradex.desc: Alttradex Token (ATXT) ran an October 2017 ICO using the OpenZeppelin RefundableCrowdsale + CryptoHuntIco template. Soft cap missed; finalize() routed the vault into refund mode. ~5 ETH still in the sibling RefundVault. Single-tx claimRefund() returns your deposit.

etcetera_ico.name: Etcetera (ERA)
etcetera_ico.desc: Etcetera (ERA) was a 2018 ICO that used the OpenZeppelin RefundableCrowdsale + CryptoHuntIco template. The sale didn't reach its goal; the contract is in refund mode. ~5 ETH still in the sibling RefundVault waiting for stragglers.

enkronos.name: Enkronos Token (ENK)
enkronos.desc: EnkronosToken (ENK) — 2018 ICO via the OpenZeppelin RefundableCrowdsale + CryptoHuntIco template. Soft cap missed. ~1 ETH still in the sibling RefundVault. Standard claimRefund() returns your original deposit.

deskbell.name: DeskBell (DBT)
deskbell.desc: DeskBell Token (DBT) ran a 2018 ICO via the OpenZeppelin RefundableCrowdsale (DeskBellPresale) template. Soft cap missed; ~4 ETH still sits in the sibling RefundVault. Standard single-tx claimRefund() returns your deposit.

quintessence.name: Quintessence (QST)
quintessence.desc: Quintessence Token (QST) — 2018 ICO using the OpenZeppelin RefundableCrowdsale (DeskBellPresale-bytecode) template. ~1.5 ETH still in the sibling RefundVault. Standard claimRefund() returns your deposit.

deck_coin.name: Deck Coin (DEK)
deck_coin.desc: Deck Coin (DEK) — 2017 ICO using the OpenZeppelin RefundableCrowdsale template. Soft cap missed. ~1.2 ETH still in the sibling RefundVault. Standard claimRefund() returns your deposit.

tokpie.name: Tokpie (TKP)
tokpie.desc: Tokpie (TKP) — exchange-token ICO from 2018 using the OpenZeppelin RefundableCrowdsale template. The crowdsale didn't reach its soft cap; ~1.2 ETH still sits in the sibling RefundVault. Standard claimRefund() returns your original deposit.

globalspy.name: GlobalSpy (SPY)
globalspy.desc: GlobalSpy (SPY) — 2018 ICO using the OpenZeppelin RefundableCrowdsale template. ~1 ETH still in the sibling RefundVault. Standard claimRefund() returns your original deposit.

bigtoken.name: BigToken (BTK)
bigtoken.desc: BigToken (BTK) ran a 2018 ICO via the BigTokenCrowdSale (OpenZeppelin RefundableCrowdsale variant). Soft cap missed (raised ~927 ETH, refunded most). ~6 ETH still in the sibling RefundVault. Standard claimRefund() returns your deposit.

winiota.name: WINiota (WIT)
winiota.desc: WINiota Token (WIT) — June 2018 ICO whose crowdsale bytecode is identical to the FNT (Friend Network) RefundableCrowdsale. Soft cap missed; ~5 ETH still in the sibling RefundVault. Standard claimRefund() returns your deposit.

grapevine.name: Grapevine (GVINE)
grapevine.desc: Grapevine (GVINE) Crowdsale — 2018 ICO using the OpenZeppelin RefundableCrowdsale template. Soft cap missed; ~1 ETH still in the sibling RefundVault. Standard claimRefund() returns your deposit.

irb.name: IRB Tokens (IRB)
irb.desc: IRB Tokens ran a 2018 pre-crowdsale via the OpenZeppelin RefundableCrowdsale template. Soft cap missed, the contract finalized into refund mode, and ~5 ETH still sits in the sibling RefundVault for the long tail of contributors. Standard single-tx claim: claimRefund() on the crowdsale returns your deposit through the vault.

cam.name: CamToken (CAM)
cam.desc: CamToken ran a 2018 ICO via the OpenZeppelin RefundableCrowdsale template. Soft cap missed and the contract finalized into refund mode; ~3 ETH still sits in the sibling RefundVault. Single-tx claim: call claimRefund() on the crowdsale and the vault returns your original deposit.

linda_pre.name: LINDA Token (Presale)
linda_pre.desc: LINDA (LNDA) ran a two-stage 2018 token sale: a presale and a main sale, both using the OpenZeppelin RefundableCrowdsale template against the same LINDA token. The presale missed its soft cap and ~3 ETH still sits in this presale RefundVault. Standard claimRefund() returns your deposit.

linda_main.name: LINDA Token (Main Sale)
linda_main.desc: The main sale stage of LINDA (LNDA), deployed alongside a separate presale contract. The main sale also missed its soft cap and finalized into refund mode; ~2.7 ETH still sits in the sibling RefundVault. Standard claimRefund() returns your deposit.

monoreto.name: Monoreto (MNR)
monoreto.desc: Monoreto ran its 2018 ICO via the OpenZeppelin RefundableCrowdsale template. Soft cap was missed and the contract finalized into refund mode; ~2.5 ETH still sits in the sibling RefundVault. Standard single-tx claim: claimRefund() on the crowdsale returns your original ETH through the vault.

ico_refund_79c5.name: Unverified ICO Refund (79c5)
ico_refund_79c5.desc: An unverified 2018 ICO crowdsale built on the OpenZeppelin RefundableCrowdsale template. Source is not published, but bytecode-level selectors match the canonical pattern, and Tenderly simulation confirms claimRefund() works for current depositors. ~1.5 ETH still sits in the sibling RefundVault.

treecoin.name: TREECHAIN NETWORK (TREECOIN)
treecoin.desc: TREECHAIN NETWORK ran a 2018 ICO via the OpenZeppelin RefundableCrowdsale template. Soft cap missed and the contract finalized into refund mode; 1 ETH remains in the sibling RefundVault for a single straggler. Standard claimRefund() returns the deposit.

lendsbay.name: Lendsbay Token (LBT)
lendsbay.desc: Lendsbay (LBT) ran a 2018 ICO via the OpenZeppelin RefundableCrowdsale template. Soft cap missed; ~0.85 ETH still in the sibling RefundVault. Standard claimRefund() returns your deposit.

furt.name: FURT COIN (FRT)
furt.desc: FURT COIN ran a 2018 ICO via the OpenZeppelin RefundableCrowdsale template. Soft cap missed; ~0.7 ETH still in the sibling RefundVault for two contributors. Standard claimRefund() returns your deposit.

kryptopy.name: Kryptopy Token (KPY)
kryptopy.desc: Kryptopy (KPY) ran a 2018 ICO via the OpenZeppelin RefundableCrowdsale template. Soft cap missed; ~0.6 ETH still in the sibling RefundVault. Standard claimRefund() returns your deposit.

digitize.name: DigitizeCoin Presale (DTZ)
digitize.desc: DigitizeCoin (DTZ) ran a 2018 presale built on the OpenZeppelin RefundableCrowdsale template. The contract uses a 3-arg TokenPurchase event variant (PallyCoin family) but the refund flow is canonical. Soft cap missed and the contract finalized into refund mode; ~1.1 ETH still sits in the sibling RefundVault. Standard claimRefund() returns your deposit.

pallycoin.name: PallyCoin (PAL)
pallycoin.desc: Merunas Grincalaitis's December 2017 PallyCoin ICO. The contract is a custom OpenZeppelin RefundableCrowdsale variant where claimRefund() additionally calls token.refundTokens(msg.sender, tokensBought[msg.sender]) — meaning you must still hold your full PAL allocation to claim. Soft cap missed and the contract finalized into refund mode. ~43 ETH remains in the RefundVault, of which ~40.6 ETH is claimable by 35 stragglers who never sold their PAL. The remaining ~2.7 ETH is locked for 9 holders who sold some PAL.

pallycoin_v2.name: PallyCoin Fork (PAL2)
pallycoin_v2.desc: A 2018 PallyCoin fork by Manoj Patidar reusing the same RefundableCrowdsale + token-gated refund pattern, but against a separate PAL2 token. Soft cap missed. ~3.77 ETH still in the RefundVault, all claimable by 5 stragglers who still hold their full PAL2 allocation. Standard claimRefund() returns your deposit; you must hold PAL2 ≥ tokensBought.

foreground.name: ForegroundTokenSale (DEAL)
foreground.desc: Foreground's DEAL token sale used a custom finite-state crowdsale (Prepared → Configured → Started → Ended → Finalized → Refunding) and held ETH directly in the crowdsale contract — no separate vault. The sale ended in the Refunding state and ~12.15 ETH still sits in the contract for 18 buyers. Single-tx claim: claimRefund() reads purchases[msg.sender].weiBalance and transfers it back. No token gating.

vuepay.name: VuePayTokenSale (VUP)
vuepay.desc: VuePay's 2017 VUP token sale used a custom crowdsale that holds ETH directly in the contract. Soft cap missed and the owner enabled refunds (allowRefund flag). ~1.2 ETH remains for 4 contributors. Single-tx claim: claimRefund() reads ETHContributed[msg.sender] and transfers it back.

chickenchef.name: ChickenChef
chickenchef.desc: ChickenChef is an abandoned SushiSwap-MasterChef-style yield farm from the 2020 DeFi summer. Pool 0 accepted direct WETH stakes (not LP tokens). The farm went silent in late 2024 and ~48 WETH still sits in the contract for 7 stakers (top staker holds 40.7 WETH). Single-tx claim: emergencyWithdraw(0) returns your full stake. The standard withdraw() also works but emergencyWithdraw is safer because it skips reward minting.

gov_treasurer.name: GovTreasurer
gov_treasurer.desc: GovTreasurer is an abandoned MasterChef-style yield farm. Pool 2 is its WETH staking pool, holding ~19 WETH for 21 stakers. The contract has been dormant since late 2023. Single-tx claim: emergencyWithdraw(2) returns your full stake.

mysteryman.name: MysteryMan
mysteryman.desc: MysteryMan is an abandoned MasterChef-style farm. Pool 0 accepted direct WETH stakes; ~4 WETH sits in the contract for 2 stakers. Single-tx claim: emergencyWithdraw(0) returns your full stake.

masterstar.name: MasterStar
masterstar.desc: MasterStar is an abandoned MasterChef-style farm. Pool 0 accepted direct WETH stakes; one staker holds the full claimable 3 WETH (the contract also holds an additional 0.1 WETH that was sent directly to the contract address and is not user-claimable). Single-tx claim: emergencyWithdraw(0) returns your full stake.

bdpmaster.name: BDPMaster
bdpmaster.desc: BDPMaster is an abandoned MasterChef-style farm. Pool 1 accepted direct WETH stakes; ~1.74 WETH sits in the contract for 8 stakers. The standard withdraw() flow is BROKEN — the contract tries to mint BDPToken rewards, but BDPToken's minter role was revoked, so withdraw() reverts with `BDPToken: cannot mint for pool`. Use emergencyWithdraw(1) instead — it skips reward minting and returns your stake cleanly.

keeperdao.name: KeeperDAO / Rook
keeperdao.desc: launched October 2020 as an on-chain liquidity underwriter backed by 3AC, Polychain and Pantera. Depositors earned yield from flash-loan fees and MEV arbitrage, receiving kTokens as LP shares. The project rebranded to Rook in late 2021, pivoted to MEV coordination, and wound down in 2022. The treasury was liquidated in April 2023 after a governance 'rage quit'. The LiquidityPoolV2 contract is still live and un-paused — depositors who still hold kETH or kwETH can redeem their proportional share of the pool (~1.08 ETH per kETH, ~1.05 WETH per kwETH, reflecting the final yield accrued before wind-down).

genesis_weth_pool.name: Pop Finance
genesis_weth_pool.blurb: Pop Finance WETH staking pool — defunct yield farm

dxmgnpool.name: Gnosis DutchX
dxmgnpool.blurb: Gnosis DutchX magnolia pool — WETH deposits for DutchX auctions

hegic_call.name: Hegic V1 Call
hegic_call.blurb: Hegic ETH call options v1 — LP liquidity tranches

hegic_eth_pool.name: Hegic V1 Pool
hegic_eth_pool.blurb: Hegic ETH options pool v1.1 — writeETH LP token holders

shrimp.name: Shrimp Finance
shrimp.desc: Shrimp Finance launched in September 2020 during DeFi Summer as a Synthetix StakingRewards fork. Users staked WETH to earn SHRIMP governance tokens. The project went defunct within weeks — the frontend is offline, the SHRIMP token worthless, and the team vanished. All staked WETH remains fully withdrawable via exit() with no time locks or token requirements.
shrimp.blurb: DeFi Summer 2020 yield farm — WETH staking pool (Synthetix fork)

bee.name: Bee2 Finance
bee.desc: Bee2 Finance launched in September 2020 during DeFi Summer as a Synthetix StakingRewards fork. Users staked WETH to earn BEE governance tokens. The project went defunct within weeks — the frontend is offline and the BEE token worthless. All staked WETH remains fully withdrawable via exit() with no time locks or token requirements.
bee.blurb: DeFi Summer 2020 yield farm — WETH staking pool (Synthetix fork)

kitten.name: Kitten Finance
kitten.desc: Kitten Finance launched in September 2020 during DeFi Summer as a Synthetix StakingRewards fork. Users staked WETH to earn KIF governance tokens. The project went defunct within weeks — the frontend is offline and the KIF token worthless. All staked WETH remains fully withdrawable via exit() with no time locks or token requirements.
kitten.blurb: DeFi Summer 2020 yield farm — WETH staking pool (Synthetix fork)

yam_weth.name: Yam Finance v1
yam_weth.desc: Yam Finance v1 launched in August 2020 as one of the first DeFi Summer yield farms. Users staked WETH to earn YAM tokens. The v1 contract had a rebase bug and the project migrated to v2/v3, abandoning this pool. All staked WETH remains fully withdrawable via exit().

spaghetti.name: Spaghetti Money
spaghetti.desc: Spaghetti Money launched in August 2020 during DeFi Summer as a yield farm fork. Users staked WETH to earn PASTA tokens. The project went defunct within days. All staked WETH remains fully withdrawable via exit().

dokidoki.name: Doki Doki Finance
dokidoki.desc: Doki Doki Finance launched in 2020 as a DeFi yield farm. Users staked WETH to earn DOKI tokens. The project is defunct. All staked WETH remains withdrawable via withdraw().

cofi.name: CoFiX Staking
cofi.desc: CoFiX (Computable Finance) launched in October 2020. Users staked CoFi tokens and earned WETH rewards. The project is defunct. Unclaimed WETH rewards are claimable via getReward().

pickle_staking.name: Pickle Staking v1
pickle_staking.desc: The Pickle Finance v1 staking contract let users stake tokens to earn WETH rewards. The project migrated to v2 and Arbitrum, abandoning this pool. Unclaimed WETH is claimable via getReward().

opyn_gamma_redeem.name: Opyn v2 Gamma
opyn_gamma_redeem.desc: Opyn v2 Gamma was the first major decentralized options protocol on Ethereum, launched in late 2020. Holders of expired in-the-money WETH-collateralized oTokens can redeem their share of the underlying collateral via the Controller contract. The protocol went silent in early 2022 — these are forgotten redemption claims from 2,613 unique holders across 38 different oToken series.

opyn_v1.name: Opyn v1
opyn_v1.desc: Opyn v1 launched in early 2020 as one of Ethereum's first decentralized options protocols. Vault owners deposited ETH as collateral to write put options (oTokens). After the options expired, vault owners could call redeemVaultBalance() to reclaim their ETH collateral. Opyn v1 was superseded by Gamma (v2) in late 2020 and the original frontend no longer supports v1 contracts. 99 vault owners across 5 expired oToken contracts still have unclaimed ETH collateral.

mesa.name: Mesa / Gnosis Protocol v1
mesa.desc: Mesa was the precursor to CowSwap (Gnosis Protocol v1), a batch-auction DEX deployed in early 2020. Users deposited tokens to trade across discrete batches and had to explicitly withdraw to retrieve them. The contract has been dormant since early 2022 but the 2-step withdraw path (requestWithdraw → wait one batch → withdraw) is still permissionless. 116 unique holders forgot to finalize withdrawals after their last trades.

uma_yield_dollar.name: UMA Yield Dollar
uma_yield_dollar.desc: UMA's ExpiringMultiParty contracts let users deposit WETH as collateral to mint synthetic tokens (yUSD, uUSDrBTC, uUSDrETH). All contracts expired in 2020-2021 and the UMA frontend no longer supports v1 settlement. Sponsors can reclaim excess collateral and synthetic token holders can redeem their share via settleExpired() on each EMP contract.

unagii.name: Unagii ETH Vault
unagii.desc: Unagii was a Yearn-style vault protocol launched in March 2021 where users deposited ETH and received uETH shares representing proportional ownership of the vault. The protocol deployed ETH into yield strategies but went dormant in late 2022. The vault contract is immutable and unpaused with no withdrawal fee. Users can burn their uETH shares to reclaim proportional ETH via withdraw().

veil_ether.name: Veil Ether
veil_ether.desc: ETH wrapper from Veil, a prediction market built on Augur and 0x that shut down in July 2019. Users deposited ETH and received Veil Ether tokens for use on the platform. The tokens can still be unwrapped 1:1 for ETH.

gavcoin.name: GavCoin
gavcoin.desc: An early Ethereum bonding curve token created in 2015. Users deposited ETH and received GavCoin tokens at a price determined by the bonding curve. Token holders can refund their tokens for ETH at their original purchase price.

celer.name: Celer Payment Channels
celer.desc: Celer was a state-channel protocol for off-chain micropayments, launched in 2019. CelerWallet is the on-chain vault; CelerLedger is the user entry point that enforces channel accounting. Celer pivoted to cBridge in 2021 and took the official CelerPay UI offline, but the contracts remained live and unpaused. Users who opened channels and never cooperatively settled can still recover their deposits via the unilateral path: intendWithdraw starts a 10,000-block (~1.4 day) dispute window; after the window any tx sender can confirm and the ETH returns to the depositor.

vader_merkle.name: Vader Protocol
vader_merkle.description: Vader Protocol was a DeFi liquidity protocol with its own stablecoin (USDV). After the protocol wound down, unclaimed WETH remains in a merkle distribution contract. Users can claim their share by providing a merkle proof.

yearn_v1_weth.name: Yearn v1 yWETH
yearn_v1_weth.desc: Yearn Finance launched the v1 yWETH vault in August 2020 as one of the first wrapped-token yield vaults. Users deposited WETH and received yWETH shares representing proportional ownership of the vault's WETH balance plus accrued yield. The yearn.fi frontend dropped v1 vault support, leaving share holders without an interface. The vault is unpaused and immutable, and all WETH sits directly in the vault (no active strategy), so yWETH holders can still redeem their shares for the underlying WETH at the current pricePerFullShare.

euler_claims.name: Euler Redemptions
euler_claims.desc: After the March 2023 Euler exploit, the recovered funds were distributed to depositors via a one-shot merkle-drop contract (EulerClaims) deployed July 2023. Each depositor's index pays out a basket of WETH, DAI, USDC, COMP, and other tokens proportional to their pre-exploit holdings. The euler-redemption-scripts repository was archived June 2024 with 1,638 indices still unclaimed. The contract is unpaused and the merkle root is final — anyone who never returned to claim can still call claimAndAgreeToTerms with the original index and proof. Each claim sends the entire basket of tokens in a single tx.

tokemak_weth.name: Tokemak v1 tWETH
tokemak_weth.desc: Tokemak launched its v1 reactor system in August 2021. The ETH pool (EthPool) accepted WETH deposits and minted tWETH shares 1:1 with WETH. Tokemak rebranded to Auto Finance in 2024 and the v1 reactor UI was retired. The contract is unpaused and tWETH holders can still exit via a 2-step flow: requestWithdrawal queues the amount at the current manager cycle, and withdraw pays out the WETH after the manager rolls cycles (~weekly). 93 tWETH holders already have matured withdrawal requests pending and can call step 2 directly.

x2y2_fee_sharing.name: X2Y2 Fee Sharing
x2y2_fee_sharing.desc: X2Y2 launched in February 2022 as an NFT marketplace competing with OpenSea. Stakers locked X2Y2 tokens into the FeeSharingSystem to earn a share of marketplace WETH fees. The x2y2.io frontend went offline and the project is dormant; the staking contract remains unpaused and immutable. Stakers can claim accrued WETH rewards at any time via harvest(), or unstake and claim simultaneously via withdraw(shares, true). About 207 WETH of accumulated fees beyond what users can claim is contract dust from accounting precision and is not user-claimable.

x2y2_presale.name: X2Y2 Presale
x2y2_presale.desc: Alongside the February 2022 launch, X2Y2 ran a signer-gated presale that sold 1,000 staking shares at 1.5 ETH each (1,500 ETH raised). Presale depositors received 15,000 X2Y2 tokens per share with a one-year linear unlock, and earned a proportional share of marketplace WETH fees directed to this contract over that period. The staking phase ended in February 2023 and the x2y2.io frontend went offline. The contract is unpaused and immutable — presale participants who still hold their share of X2Y2 tokens can call harvest() at any time to claim their pending WETH rewards. Depositors who already called withdraw() (which internally harvests and claims the X2Y2 tokens) no longer have any pending rewards. About 121 WETH in the contract is math-stranded from that pattern and can only be swept by the X2Y2 admin multisig via treasuryWithdraw().

metadrop_anata.name: Metadrop: Anata
metadrop_anata.desc: In April 2022, Metadrop ran a sealed-bid auction for the Anata NFT collection — 1,920 items at a uniform clearing price. Bidders submitted ETH-backed orders above a minimum reserve; those who bid above the clearing price won NFTs while the difference between their bid and the clearing price was refundable via a merkle-gated claim. Metadrop set the refund merkle root on-chain and shut down metadrop.com shortly after. The contract is immutable with no deadline — 120 bidders across 197.33 ETH have never claimed their refund. The root is frozen on-chain and matching proofs were reconstructed from archived frontend JS bundles.

metadrop_webaverse.name: Metadrop: Webaverse
metadrop_webaverse.desc: In August 2022, Metadrop ran a sealed-bid auction for the Webaverse metaverse NFT collection — 7,870 items at a uniform clearing price. Bidders submitted ETH-backed orders above a reserve; winners received NFTs and the difference between their bid and the clearing price was refundable via a merkle-gated claim. Webaverse as a project went dormant in late 2023 and metadrop.com shut down around the same time. The contract is immutable with no deadline — 90 bidders across 134.15 ETH have never claimed their refund. The on-chain root is frozen and matching proofs were reconstructed from archived frontend JS bundles.

gnosis_auction.name: Gnosis Auction
gnosis_auction.desc: Gnosis Auction (EasyAuction) is a batch auction platform launched by Gnosis in 2021 for fair token launches. Bidders submitted WETH orders at their chosen price; orders below the clearing price were refunded. The gnosis-auction.eth.limo frontend is dead, but the contract is immutable with no deadline. Unfilled and partially-filled orders can still be claimed via claimFromParticipantOrder(auctionId, orders[]), which returns the unfilled WETH portion of each order.

nomad_recovery.name: Nomad Bridge Recovery
nomad_recovery.desc: After the August 2022 Nomad Bridge exploit ($190M drained in a chain of ~300 copycat transactions), whitehats returned roughly $40M to a Nomad-controlled address. The Nomad Foundation deployed an AllowListNFTRecoveryAccountant contract (0xa4B86BcbB18639D8e708d6163a0c734aFcDB770c) that mints one ERC-721 NFT per affected (user, asset) pair — someone bridged WETH + USDC + WBTC pre-hack would receive three NFTs. Each NFT represents a fixed pro-rata share of whatever the Accountant holds of that specific asset. Users call recover(tokenId) to claim. Two gates apply: the caller must own the NFT, AND the caller must be on the allowList[] (maintained by the Nomad team via off-chain KYC verification). The contract is still active — the official recovery dashboard was discontinued but the contract itself is immutable and permissionless for allowlisted holders. About 95% of the WETH pool (46 of 48 claimable WETH) is held by allowlisted addresses who have never called recover(). Victim research data: github.com/nomad-xyz/hack-data.
nomad_recovery.steps_note: One recover(tokenId) call per NFT. Each NFT pays out a single asset (WETH, USDC, USDT, DAI, FRAX, WBTC, CQT, or FXS). Users with multiple NFTs must call recover() separately for each. Non-allowlisted NFT holders must contact the Nomad team to be added before they can claim.
